2078: Archaeology as a Science
2079: 
2080: There are both positive and negative views of science. In the positive
2081: view, science is wonderful. It has given us modern medicine, transport,
2082: a level of material affluence few people would wish to give up. We know
2083: the way the world is because scientists tell us so. Scientists are so
2084: powerful, hold such a grip over our beliefs and sentiments, that we
2085: believe them even when our strongest intuitions tell us otherwise, when
2086: boarding an aeroplane for example. But in the negative view science can
2087: be disturbing and alarming. It can be dangerous, it can offend human
2088: sensibilities. Science has moral limits: look what happened to Doctor
2089: Frankenstein (figure 3.1).
2090: 
2091: Both positive and negative views of science are culturally loaded. To
2092: clarify, Science is viewed in a certain way within Western culture. The
2093: sociologist Auguste Comte suggested that science is a central
2094: institution of authority in the modern world, just as the Catholic
2095: Church acted as an authority for the people of medieval Europe.
2096: 
2097: Modern Western society is partly based on the idea of Science with a
2098: capital S. The term ‘scientific’ is used as a form of approbation, and
2099: that of ‘unscientific’ as one of abuse. But Westerners are often less
2100: sure what they actually mean by the term ‘Science’, which I have
2101: dignified here with a capital S to distinguish the image from the
2102: reality. What is Science, are there different forms of Science, and to
2103: which forms (if any) can or should archaeology strive to approximate?
2104: 
2105: This was one of the problems that faced the New Archaeology as it
2106: matured, and it is a problem which archaeology still faces today. As we
2107: have seen, ‘we must be more scientific’ was a good slogan, and
2108: concentrated (and continues to concentrate) appropriate criticism on
2109: limitations in the approaches and methods of culture historians. But New
2110: Archaeology found that deciding what it actually meant by its slogan was
2111: more difficult.
2112: 
2113: One of the ways in which archaeology has become more ‘scientific’ in the
2114: last 50 years is in its techniques. The period after the Second World
2115: War saw an explosion in the number and range of ‘science-based’
2116: techniques used in archaeology. These included the use of computers, the
2117: study of environmental remains, pollen diagrams, dating techniques such
2118: as carbon-14 and dendrochronology, soil geomorphology, paleopathology,
2119: and so on. Though I did not discuss these at any length in the last
2120: chapter, for many people, such as David Clarke, this development and
2121: growth in use of scientific techniques was a central part of the New
2122: Archaeology. In the last decade, advances in DNA research, chemical
2123: analysis of human and animal bones, advanced geophysical techniques,
2124: geographical information systems, virtual reality, and many other
2125: developments have continued to unfold.
2126: 
2127: Figure 3.1 Lon Chaney, Jr (left) and Lionel Atwill in Man Made Monster
2128: (1941, Universal): still from Everson, W. K. 1974 Classics of the Horror
2129: Film (Secaucus, NJ, The Citadel Press), 152–3
2130: 
2131: [c03_image001.jpg]
2132: 
2133: This development of scientific techniques, and their place as an ever
2134: more important part of the discipline, has led to a problem of technical
2135: specialization. An archaeologist in 1950 had to have an understanding of
2136: some statistics, and the principles of geology. Sixty years later, she
2137: or he does not necessarily have to know the practical details of a
2138: technique, but does have to know how to use a pollen diagram, how to
2139: manipulate statistical packages on computers, how to interpret and
2140: understand the significance of a radiocarbon determination or a
2141: tree-ring date, and at the very least how to understand and use the
2142: findings of a range of other very complex and specialized techniques. As
2143: a result, it can be argued that archaeology is a much more fragmented
2144: discipline, and less of a unified and homogenous community, than it was
2145: 50 years ago.
2146: 
2147: In this sense the general atmosphere of the practice of archaeology has
2148: become more ‘scientific’. Where archaeologists used to work in dusty
2149: backrooms full of old junk, their offices are often now part of suites
2150: of laboratories (often taken over second-hand from other, more
2151: ‘respectable’ physicists, biologists or chemists who have moved into
2152: newer, smarter accommodation). In many universities, particularly in
2153: North America, smart white labcoats have succeeded frayed tweed jackets
2154: with elbow-patches as the uniform of the academic archaeologist. Perhaps
2155: more importantly, key career targets for many academics are now articles
2156: in peer-reviewed scientific journals as much as the more traditional
2157: synthetic monograph or site report.
2158: 
2159: The growth of ‘science’ has led to changes in the pattern of funding of
2160: academic archaeology. In the USA, archaeology is funded under the aegis
2161: of the National Science Foundation. In Britain and in much of the rest
2162: of Europe, funding is often predominantly ‘humanistic’ (for example,
2163: through the British Arts and Humanities Research Council) or tied to
2164: ‘culture’ or ‘heritage’, though the situation varies widely in different
2165: national contexts and is subject to rapid change.
2166: 
2167: In its essentials, however, the use of scientific techniques does not in
2168: itself imply the use of ‘science’ as a distinctive approach to finding
2169: out about the past. David Clarke wrote that the use of scientific
2170: techniques ‘no more make archaeology into a science than a wooden leg
2171: turns a man into a tree’ (Clarke, 1978: 465). For the traditional
2172: archaeologist Jacquetta Hawkes, archaeology remains essentially a
2173: humanistic, not a scientific, pursuit: ‘however scientific the methods
2174: employed, the final aims are historical’ (Hawkes, 1968: 257).
2175: 
2176: To clarify, archaeologists use an increasing battery of techniques to
2177: assist in dating, the environment, geophysical techniques, and so on.
2178: These techniques have given us more and more data of potential use in
2179: the study of the past. But we are still left with the gulf between
2180: present and past discussed in chapter 2 and presented in figure 2.1.
2181: ‘Science-based’ techniques accumulate even more data in the present. If
2182: that gulf between present and past remains unbridgeable, then the
2183: statements archaeologists make about the past remain ‘unscientific’
2184: however clean their white coats are or how much money they spend on the
2185: many machines that go ping! in laboratories. If, on the other hand, the
2186: gulf can be bridged securely, using the methods of the natural sciences,
2187: then archaeology really can be called scientific, regardless of whether
2188: or not archaeologists wear white coats and use lots of expensive
2189: equipment.
2190: 
2191: Definitions of Science
2192: 
2193: New Archaeology suggested the use of ‘science’ as the solution to the
2194: problem of inference, discussed in the last chapter. If archaeologists
2195: have no reliable means of assessing how valid their arguments really
2196: were, it is only natural to look at how natural scientists do it. The
2197: natural sciences of physics, chemistry, biology, after all, appear in
2198: the view of many to have demonstrable and indeed spectacular success in
2199: terms of their ability to accurately describe and explain the world when
2200: compared for example to other forms of thought such as magic or
2201: mysticism.
2202: 
2203: Such an argument is reinforced by the disciplinary success of science in
2204: the ‘real world’. Whatever one’s views of science as a theory, there is
2205: no disputing its long-term success as a discipline. Scientists are often
2206: well funded compared to humanistic scholars, and often appear to have
2207: the ear of government and an influential role in setting public policy.
2208: 
2209: In one sense there is no argument about whether archaeologists should be
2210: scientific. If science is about the rational accumulation of knowledge,
2211: assessed in rigorous, systematic ways, then all archaeologists would
2212: consider themselves to be scientists. (At least, archaeologists would
2213: all claim to be scientists, though our opponents always seem to be
2214: lacking in rigour, system or method.) Science in this broad sense is
2215: often referred to by the German term Wissenschaft. Even the most fervent
2216: opponent of ‘archaeologyas-science’ would probably consider themselves
2217: scientists in this sense.
2218: 
2219: Positivism
2220: 
2221: There are, however, more narrow definitions of what science is (figure
2222: 3.2). One of these definitions is called positivism, which is another
2223: word with several meanings. Theorists confusingly use the word
2224: ‘positivism’ in different senses in different contexts. Here, I shall
2225: isolate two.
2226: 
2227: 1 A set of beliefs about how we should conduct scientific enquiry
2228: 
2229: These beliefs include:
2230: 
2231: (a) The idea that scientists should separate theory from method, however
2232: method is defined. If we have two or more competing theories to explain
2233: a phenomenon, then we have to have some kind of independent and neutral
2234: method to judge which theory is the better. It is no good trying to
2235: conduct such an independent test if our method is not independent and
2236: neutral, but is predicated on one of the theories being true. This is
2237: why many archaeologists would vehemently oppose the very broad
2238: definition of theory I adopted in chapter 1 as ‘the order you put facts
2239: in’, as it does have the implication of making a clear and strict
2240: separation of this kind difficult if not impossible.
2241: 
2242: Figure 3.2 A selective diagram showing some schools within the
2243: philosophy of science
2244: 
2245: [c03_image002.jpg]
2246: 
2247: (b) The separation of the context of the discovery of an idea from the
2248: context of its evaluation. It doesn’t matter whether the law of gravity
2249: came to us sitting under an apple tree or in a book-lined office or in a
2250: drugcrazed hallucination; what is important is that the existence of
2251: such a law is evaluated scientifically separately from this context.
2252: Again, we might get some perfectly good ideas about processes in the
2253: prehistoric past from comparative ethnography or from a novel or a
2254: dream; what is important, in this view, is that we then test the ideas
2255: against the archaeological record to see whether they are valid or not.
2256: 
2257: Archaeologists will often claim, therefore, that they are using a
2258: particular idea or model only for heuristic or learning purposes; that
2259: is, they find the model of value in throwing up new hypotheses or
2260: possible interpretations. They might well insist that such hypotheses or
2261: interpretations need then to be formally tested or evaluated before they
2262: can be considered to be of lasting value.
2263: 
2264: (c) A generalizing explanation is the only valid form. Here, generality
2265: is closely linked to the importance of predictability and testability.
2266: Results have to be predictable and repeated for scientific validity.
2267: Scien tists can test the law of gravity by observing apples dropping
2268: from trees again and again and again. The law of gravity is framed as a
2269: general proposition that has predictable consequences, all other things
2270: being equal.
2271: 
2272: If an explanation is not generalizing, if it cannot suggest a consistent
2273: pattern of results, it will not be predictable and therefore will not be
2274: amenable to testing again and again.
2275: 
2276: (d) Untestable statements are outside the domain of science. In less
2277: extreme forms of positivism, this does not mean that untestable
2278: statements are unimportant. For example, moral and metaphysical
2279: questions may be untestable but central to our lives. Whether God exists
2280: or whether slavery or child labour are morally wrong are clearly
2281: important issues for individuals to discuss and act upon as human beings
2282: and communities; but they are not scientifically testable one way or the
2283: other. Science therefore, in this view, has nothing to say about them.
2284: 
2285: (e) It follows from (d) that scientific thought should be independent of
2286: value judgements and political action. The threat to use nuclear weapons
2287: or investment in countries with repressive regimes may or may not be
2288: morally or politically wrong; we all, scientists or non-scientists, make
2289: value judgements concerning these matters as human beings living in the
2290: world today. But the scientist cannot bring these judgements into the
2291: methods of his or her work. She or he might comment on them in other
2292: spheres, even campaign against nuclear weapons or repressive regimes,
2293: but must draw a clear line between (morally neutral) scientific
2294: knowledge and moral judgement and action.
2295: 
2296: Positivism in this sense has tended to be linked with a specific method
2297: of testing propositions, called the Hypothetico-deductive-nomological
2298: model, or HDN for short. The HDN model suggests that one proceeds as a
2299: scientist by taking a specific hypothesis and testing it. The deductions
2300: one makes from the result of the test should then be used to produce
2301: generalizing explanations (‘nomothetic’ or ‘nomological’ means
2302: generalizing). The need to test specific hypotheses, rather than just
2303: dig sites because we might find something interesting or because they
2304: are particularly big or beautiful, was an issue discussed in the last
2305: chapter.
2306: 
2307: But positivism also, confusingly, has a second set of meanings attached
2308: to it.
2309: 
2310: 2 The belief that the social sciences, including archaeology, should try
2311: to follow the historical path of development of the natural sciences
2312: 
2313: This argument was advanced for sociology by the nineteenth century
2314: French social philosopher Auguste Comte, who was one of the key figures
2315: in the development of the human sciences in general. Comte looked to the
2316: example of biology. Before the eighteenth century, biology had, in
2317: Comte’s view, been a speculative and unsystematic pursuit at a time when
2318: the scientific techniques and methods of physics and chemistry were
2319: already established. By modelling its disciplinary rules on the ‘hard
2320: sciences’ of physics and chemistry, however, biology had freed itself
2321: from the shackles of prescientific beliefs and become a rigorous
2322: science. Comte suggested that social sciences like sociology could
2323: follow a similar route, moving from their present unscientific state to
2324: that of a natural science.
2325: 
2326: New and processual archaeology can clearly be identified with much of
2327: the agenda outlined above. New Archaeology tried to generalize; it tried
2328: to adopt a HDN model of testing; its whole project was that of Comte’s,
2329: that of leading archaeology along an analogous path to that of the
2330: natural sciences to a mature, rigorous, self-critical discipline. For
2331: David Clarke (1973), this was part of archaeology’s ‘loss of innocence’.
2332: 
2333: Examples
2334: 
2335: For New Archaeology, then, and for much of ‘processual archaeology’
2336: today, we should adopt a broadly positivist definition of what we do. We
2337: should be trying to test or evaluate hypotheses about the past, and to
2338: draw generalizations from those hypotheses.
2339: 
2340: Such a model might proceed as follows:
2341: 
2342: 1 Hypothesis: early states involve differential access to resources, in
2343: other words that élites have greater access to basic goods (a hypothesis
2344: suggested maybe by political anthropologists).
2345: 
2346: 2 Test: dig a cemetery from an early state society and chemically
2347: analyse the human bones.
2348: 
2349: 3 Deduction: the élite ate more meat, so we deduce that they did indeed
2350: have greater access to nutrition.
2351: 
2352: 4 Generalization: early states do have such differential access, subject
2353: to further testing, other examples from other cultures at the same phase
2354: of social development, etc.
2355: 
2356: Another example might be hunter-gatherers and social complexity:
2357: 
2358: 1 Hypothesis: that hunter-gatherer groups adapt to marginal environments
2359: in part through greater economic specialization and social complexity
2360: (suggested perhaps by study of modern ethnographies).
2361: 
2362: 2 Test: take a marginal environment such as Upper Palaeolithic Europe in
2363: the last Ice Age, and look for different types of site suggesting task
2364: differentiation, the presence or absence of trade goods suggesting
2365: social alliances, etc.
2366: 
2367: 3 Deduction: that social alliance and logistical hunting strategies are
2368: responses to harsher climates.
2369: 
2370: 4 Generalization: there is a positive correlation between increasingly
2371: marginal environments and greater social complexity.
2372: 
2373: Note that in both these examples there is a tendency to generalize.
2374: General statements tend to emerge from the discussion: for example, that
2375: increasing complexity of trading networks tends to be linked to élites
2376: and thus to development of social ranking, whether this is in late
2377: prehistoric Europe, Mesoamerica or Polynesia. Look at the titles of some
2378: typical edited volumes in the processual mould, in which different
2379: articles often based on data sets from around the world and different
2380: time periods focus on certain processes which are held to be general and
2381: cross-cultural: Ranking, Resource and Exchange … Specialisation,
2382: Exchange and Complex Societies … Heterarchy and the Analysis of Complex
2383: Societies …
2384: 
2385: Such generalizations are what is meant by a ‘softer’ approach to
2386: covering laws than that taken in other areas of the natural sciences. In
2387: its first phases, some New Archaeologists searched for ‘laws’ of human
2388: behaviour, on the model of the hard sciences. It has proven to be very
2389: difficult to come up with ‘laws’ for past human behaviour akin to the
2390: law of gravity (we will discuss the attempt to create a unified field
2391: with biology, itself a ‘softer’ science than chemistry or physics,
2392: through the application of Darwinian ideas in chapter 10). This does not
2393: mean, however, that the production of generalizations about past human
2394: behaviour is necessarily an invalid or an unimportant goal – indeed, for
2395: the reasons discussed above, for a discipline that sees itself as a
2396: science in which propositions are testable, generalization or general
2397: theory has to be a central component of archaeology.
2398: 
2399: Objections to ‘Science’
2400: 
2401: We noted that if part of the appeal of ‘Science’ comes from its cultural
2402: value in modern society, so opposition to ‘Science’ is similarly
2403: culturally loaded. For some, archaeology can never be scientific;
2404: rather, it is a humanistic discipline. Rather than being about white
2405: labcoats and generalizations, it is about what it means to be a
2406: civilized human being. Archaeology in this view is a noble, aesthetic,
2407: romantic pursuit into the essence of humanity and civilization,
2408: untainted by the vulgarities of labcoats and test-tubes. In some
2409: conceptions of this view, human beings are special because they are
2410: unique and unpredictable: they simply can not be generalized about or
2411: subsumed in statistics. Further, many of the things that are most
2412: ‘distinctively human’ and therefore most worthy of humanistic study
2413: (great art and literature, for example) are precisely those that in this
2414: view are least amenable to scientific enquiry.
2415: 
2416: Such a view is easy to ridicule, and it certainly does not stand up to
2417: more than a few seconds of critical thought (if humans really were
2418: unique and unpredictable, opinion polls would produce completely random
2419: results and any attempt at social or economic policy by government
2420: agencies would start and end in chaos), but is a very popular and
2421: emotionally powerful one. Human beings often like to think that they are
2422: unique, are beyond statistics; but wishing something does not make it
2423: true.
2424: 
2425: Such a view, or a more sophisticated version of it, is nevertheless
2426: foundational to many elements of modern archaeology. For example,
2427: Classical archaeology was in its historical origins an outgrowth of the
2428: study of the ‘Classics’. The texts, history and monuments of Greece and
2429: Rome were studied because they were held to constitute the foundations
2430: of civilized and humane behaviour, for example in the origins of the
2431: idea of democracy in fifth century BC Athens. Further, for men of the
2432: upper classes in the Renaissance, Classical texts provided role models –
2433: how great men should act, what it meant to be civilized. Very few
2434: Classical archaeologists would subscribe to such an unmodified view of
2435: the rationale of their discipline today, but elements of such a view are
2436: nevertheless still very powerful, in that they are inscribed into the
2437: institutional set-up and identity of Classics and of Classical
2438: archaeology, for instance in the separation between centres of ‘pre- and
2439: proto-history’ and ‘Classical archaeology’ in many institutions in
2440: continental Europe.
2441: 
2442: A series of more intellectually powerful objections to the positivist
2443: conception of archaeology-as-science are commonly brought up. I shall
2444: deal with them in turn.
2445: 
2446: 1 Science is based on testing and observation of results. The past,
2447: however, is dead and gone. We will never be able to directly observe it.
2448: Can we ever test propositions about the past as we can (it is argued) in
2449: science? Possibly not; this is a question I shall address below and more
2450: particularly in the next chapter. It is worth noting that archaeology in
2451: this respect shares some of the interpretative problems with other
2452: sciences that interpret phenomena observed now in terms of processes and
2453: events that occurred in the very distant past. Geology interprets data
2454: observed today (layers, sediments) in terms of processes that happened
2455: millions of years ago; astronomy observes phenomena many light-years
2456: away. In both cases, it is possible to argue that ‘direct’ observation
2457: of phenomena is problematic.
2458: 
2459: 2 Atoms, chemicals and even biological organisms can be seen as objects,
2460: insensate things that behave in predictable ways. Human behaviour,
2461: however, can be seen as purposive or intentional. In other words, human
2462: actions can only be fully explained by reference to the ideas and
2463: intentions that humans have. Ideas and intentions do not have any
2464: physical existence (except, arguably, as electronic traces in the
2465: brain); they only exist as thoughts, between our ears. They can not be
2466: directly observed or ‘measured’ by the archaeologist. Interpretation in
2467: archaeology is therefore, in this view, always hermeneutic (about ideas,
2468: meanings and symbols) in nature rather than scientific. This argument,
2469: or variants thereof, is one of the central planks of interpretive
2470: archaeology.
2471: 
2472: Kuhn and Feyerabend
2473: 
2474: The final and perhaps most serious objection to positivism is that it is
2475: not a theory but a myth. In other words, positivism is an ideal model of
2476: scientific philosophy, but is a bit of a fraud in practice: it gives a
2477: false idea of what scientists actually do. To ask archaeology to follow
2478: the rules of positivist science is therefore asking them to follow a
2479: chimera.
2480: 
2481: There is much confusion here over whether positivism describes what
2482: scientists actually do, or whether it is merely an ideal statement of
2483: what they should do. Some philosophers claim that it is merely an ideal
2484: statement. If positivism is only an ideal account, what do scientists
2485: actually get up to in their laboratories?. Many have argued that what
2486: counts as an ‘observation’ and ‘fact’ is decided at least in part by
2487: social rules, not by purely objective enquiry however defined. Instead,
2488: scientific debates are decided through processes that are thoroughly
2489: enmeshed in social relations, not disengaged from society.
2490: 
2491: Two philosophers of science are often cited in non-positivist accounts
2492: of science. The first is Thomas Kuhn. Kuhn argued that the history of
2493: science is not just a simple progressively upward success story of
2494: hypothesis testing leading to expanded knowledge. Rather, it is one of
2495: successive paradigms. A paradigm is much deeper than a single theory or
2496: proposition: it is a set of beliefs about the way the world works that
2497: underlies the whole everyday process of science very deeply, so deeply
2498: that it is rarely overtly put into words. During a period of ‘normal
2499: science’ in Kuhn’s terms, one paradigm enjoys such undisputed sway over
2500: science that its assumptions are rarely even stated explicitly, let
2501: alone questioned or debated. Everyone just gets on with scientific
2502: labour within the assumptions of the paradigm.
2503: 
2504: Kuhn suggested that a paradigm will break down eventually. When it does,
2505: it is eventually replaced by a new paradigm in a ‘paradigm shift’. Such
2506: a period of paradigm shift is intense, emotional, revolutionary, rather
2507: than cool and detached; a spirit of revolutionary fervour militates
2508: against ‘rational’ argument.
2509: 
2510: The story of science, then, is one of successive paradigms succeeding
2511: one another via revolutionary upheavals rather than of steady
2512: accumulation of better and better knowledge of the world around us.
2513: Colin Renfrew and others argued in the 1960s and 1970s that the New
2514: Archaeology represented a ‘paradigm shift’ and that we should therefore
2515: expect processual archaeology to settle down to a new period of ‘normal
2516: science’ (something that, as we shall see, has arguably not happened).
2517: There was an irony here, in that Renfrew was citing with approval a
2518: philosopher of science whose work actually tended to undermine many of
2519: the positivist assumptions of New Archaeology.
2520: 
2521: Kuhn himself repudiated the idea that science didn’t progress; his view
2522: was that paradigms broke down under the steadily increasing weight of
2523: contradictory evidence, and that each paradigm was a successively better
2524: and more accurate way of looking at the world. Renfrew and others agree
2525: with him in this respect. Other scholars, however, took Kuhn’s ideas and
2526: extended them in ways that Kuhn himself opposed. They drew different and
2527: more wide-ranging morals from his work: in particular, that:
2528: 
2529: 1 What constituted valid ‘facts’ or ‘observations’ depended on the
2530: prevailing paradigm. In other words, what was relevant evidence, and
2531: what was ruled out of court, depended at least in part on one’s initial
2532: paradigmatic assumptions. In this view, then, facts are therefore always
2533: theorydependent.
2534: 
2535: 2 Social and political forces, not just disinterested scientific
2536: enquiry, played a key part in prompting and shaping paradigm shifts.
2537: 
2538: Kuhn’s main influence on the philosophy of science, therefore, has been
2539: in a move away from positivist assumptions about the nature of
2540: scientific progress, a questioning of the use of a single positivist
2541: method in scientific enquiry, and a scepticism towards the narrative of
2542: science as an unqualified success story.
2543: 
2544: The second key figure is Paul Feyerabend, whose description of the way
2545: science actually works is always popular since it consists of two words:
2546: Anything Goes. Feyerabend questioned whether science had a single method
2547: at all. He suggested that if we looked historically at the development
2548: of science, change in scientific beliefs had always been marked by a
2549: diversity of methods.
2550: 
2551: Feyerabend used the example of the astronomer Galileo, who, he argued,
2552: moved science forward by breaking all the rules, using political
2553: rhetoric and appeals to emotion and working within the social and
2554: political context of seventeenth-century Italy: ‘Galileo prevails
2555: because of his style and his clever techniques of persuasion, because he
2556: writes in Italian rather than in Latin, and because he appeals to people
2557: who are temperamentally opposed to the old ideas and the standards of
2558: learning connected with them’ (Feyerabend 1988: 13).
2559: 
2560: For Feyerabend, the lesson of the history of scientific ideas was that
2561: to achieve better results, scientists should reject the use of a single
2562: method. Rather, we should encourage an unlimited diversity of methods
2563: and groups within sciences, and to allow and even encourage
2564: ‘unscientific’ strategies such as appeals to emotion. Feyerabend
2565: stressed the social and political forces that, in his view, actively
2566: shape scientific enquiry. He pointed out that the relative merits of
2567: scientific ideas are not tested out on a level playing field. He
2568: stressed what he saw as the enormously powerful nature of scientific
2569: institutions and argued that they hid their partiality behind their
2570: ‘objective’ façade. Positivism in the form of a single method, in
2571: Feyerabend’s view, masks ‘institutional intimidation’ by pretending to
2572: be neutral, hinders the development of science, and encourages scientism
2573: (the belief that Science is not simply different, but superior) and the
2574: ‘cult of the expert’.
2575: 
2576: Social Constructivism
2577: 
2578: It is important to stress that Kuhn and Feyerabend saw their work as
2579: describing what scientists actually do, rather than as a prescription of
2580: what they should do. Their work encouraged the development of
2581: sociological studies of science – that is, ethnographic or historical
2582: examinations of what actually happens in the scientific process. In the
2583: 1990s, these studies coalesced into a school of thought calling itself
2584: social constructivism or constructionism: the belief that scientific
2585: knowledge is not purely objective, but is at least partly or entirely
2586: socially constructed. (‘Weak social constructivism’ holds that it is
2587: partly socially constructed; ‘strong social constructivism’ that it is
2588: entirely so.)
2589: 
2590: Examples from the natural sciences include:
2591: 
2592: 1 Quarks. Andrew Pickering (1984) discussed the ‘discovery’ of this
2593: subatomic particle. He stressed that rather than quarks ‘really
2594: existing’ and waiting to be discovered, a full account of the scientific
2595: activity leading to the definition of quarks must look at such things as
2596: the decisions of funding bodies, personal and institutional
2597: relationships, university and state politics, and so on. Quarks in this
2598: view were not ‘discovered’; rather, they were actively ‘constructed’ by
2599: the scientists involved. And this construction was a social one; it
2600: depended on the interaction of scientists, politicians, funding
2601: agencies, universities and the wider public.
2602: 
2603: 2 Races. Scientific definitions of different races in physical
2604: anthropology are, it can be argued, impositions on a continuum of
2605: physical types across the globe. Where, then, do classifications by
2606: physical anthropologists into different races come from? It can be
2607: argued that scientific classifications of ‘types’ are not neutral
2608: devices for objective classification, but are in fact derived from the
2609: end points of trade routes from Europe to and from the ‘colonies’ in the
2610: nineteenth century, that is at precisely that time when the basic
2611: classifications of physical anthropology were being formed: Northern
2612: Europeans, West Africans, Hong Kong Chinese, American ‘Indians’… If
2613: social constructivism is a correct analysis of science, then the
2614: question ‘should archaeology be a science or a humanity?’ is rendered
2615: meaningless. Constructivism questions the claim of science to be a
2616: distinctive form of knowledge in the first place. It therefore denies
2617: any basic, a priori differences between science and non-science. It
2618: suggests that we should look at what human beings do in laboratories in
2619: the same way as we look at what they do in other walks of life.
2620: 
2621: Social constructivism unleashed what was often a very angry and bitter
2622: debate between different groups of scientists and others, often referred
2623: to retrospectively as the ‘science wars’. ‘Strong’ social
2624: constructivists were seen by their opponents as threatening the very
2625: foundations of rationality and academic enquiry itself. Since the 1990s,
2626: the intensity and ferocity of this debate has subsided on both sides,
2627: though the issues at stake remain fundamental and we will return to them
2628: in future chapters.
2629: 
2630: All this is fine, but I find it difficult to understand how this might
2631: apply to archaeology. Which version of science do archaeologists
2632: currently prefer? Do they come down on one side or the other of the
2633: debate?
2634: 
2635: There is no one answer to your question; you will have to read the
2636: literature, and then make up your own mind.
2637: 
2638: Different archaeologists advocate different forms of scientific
2639: philosophy. Shanks and Tilley argued in the late 1980s that positivism’s
2640: stress on separating method from theory is fraudulent, because theory
2641: and method can never be separated. Like Feyerabend, they see positivism
2642: as a covert, underhand way of controlling what we can and cannot say and
2643: do in archaeology. Positivism for them is an archaeology of NO. They
2644: wrote in a spirit of sarcasm:
2645: 
2646: It doesn’t matter what you say as long as you say it in the right way;
2647: as long as you conform to the rules of positivist/empiricist discourse,
2648: rational method; as long as what you say is reasonable, not fantasy or
2649: extreme, is open to ‘testing’ against the data, is not overtly
2650: political, is not subjective. And if you transgress these laws of
2651: discourse, the epistemology … police are waiting. (Shanks and Tilley
2652: 1992: 23)
2653: 
2654: For Shanks and Tilley, the way we write about the past is largely about
2655: political rules. They want to unmask the political nature of these
2656: rules.
2657: 
2658: Opponents replied: if we abandon positivist criteria, what alternatives
2659: are there? If we accept such views, aren’t beliefs in magic or in
2660: mystical forces just as ‘scientific’ as rational debate?
2661: 
2662: As a result of these debates spilling over into archaeology in the 1980s
2663: and 1990s, there has been a growing interest in philosophies or
2664: conceptions of scientific method that acknowledge that all
2665: archaeological data are socially constructed in some sense, but which
2666: try to move beyond a simppmple either/or position. The ‘science wars’
2667: seemed to many to be based on a false choice, an unnecessary opposition
2668: or polarity – either Science had to be absolutely objective and
2669: untainted by cultural context, or it had to be nothing more than one
2670: more discourse among many, including magical and superstitious beliefs.
2671: Both of these views or positions are, in the view of many, including
2672: myself, unsatisfactory. For archaeologists this translates into the
2673: question: if we have to reject a crude or naïve positivism, can we
2674: nevertheless salvage something from the wreckage – keep hold of some
2675: idea of rigour and method?
2676: 
2677: For example, Alison Wylie has combined an insistence that science is
2678: inevitably value-laden in different ways, with an exploration of ways in
2679: which in practice, archaeologists do move back and forth in an
2680: empirically informed manner between data and interpretation. More
2681: broadly, there has I think been a subtle shift in the language and tone
2682: of different strands of processual and postprocessual archaeology. Fewer
2683: processualists talk of ‘testing’ their arguments, but they do insist on
2684: proper and rigorous ‘evaluation’. Conversely, interpretive and
2685: ‘postprocessual’ archaeologists acknowledge and affirm that arguments do
2686: need to be evaluated with reference to the data, and that the data at
2687: the very least form a network of resistance.
2688: 
2689: At the same time, this theoretical position has been complemented and
2690: strengthened by practical developments. There has been a growing body of
2691: work that has used ‘scientific’ techniques however defined to look at
2692: interpretive questions. For example, archaeologists have used human
2693: skeletal material to explore issues of gender, the body and identity.
2694: Materials science has been deployed to examine the socially constrained
2695: choices that craftspeople made in the production of pottery and other
2696: artefacts. Techniques of archaeological computing such as geographical
2697: information systems have been used to explore the experience of place.
2698: These are all themes we will examine in later chapters.
2699: 
2700: But if you find the same range of views within archaeology, it is also
2701: worth pointing out that the debate over scientific method in archaeology
2702: is a re-run of a similar debate in virtually every other social science.
2703: Behavioural psychologists claim that thoughts are beyond the domain of
2704: science, so we should concentrate on people’s behaviour. In sociology,
2705: Auguste Comte and Emile Durkheim laid out sociological method in a
2706: positivistic framework; their aim was to construct a science of society
2707: in which predictions and generalizations could be made about social
2708: phenomena in the same way as natural phenomena. The sociologist Anthony
2709: Giddens and others have, in opposition to Durkheim and Comte, argued for
2710: the impossibility of a neutral, value-free science of society. Similar
2711: debates occur within all those disciplines classified as social
2712: sciences: history, linguistics, economics, politics. Many feminists
2713: claim that Science is not merely political, but that it is moreover a
2714: male construct: the rules of ‘rational method’ mask male bias behind a
2715: cloak of objectivity.
2716: 
2717: Archaeologists can comfort themselves that their epistemological worries
2718: are at least shared across the domain of the social sciences. Whether
2719: this makes them easier to resolve is open to doubt.
2720: 
2721: 4
2722: 
2723: Middle-range Theory, Ethnoarchaeology and Material Culture Studies
2724: 
2725: In this chapter, we return to figure 2.1 and the gap between present and
2726: past, ‘data’ and ‘interpretation’, but we take a rather different route
2727: from chapter 3 in asking how archaeologists bridge this gap. We have all
2728: this stuff, this archaeological material – pots, stones, bones. It
2729: exists here in the present; how do we get it to tell us about the past?
2730: 
2731: If any statement about the past is unavoidably made in the present, it
2732: is also unavoidably an analogy. An analogy is the use of information
2733: derived from one context, in this case usually the present, to explain
2734: data found in another context, in this case the past.
2735: 
2736: To clarify: all archaeologists of whatever theoretical stripe make a
2737: link between present and past by using analogies. We always make an
2738: assumption that things in the past were like – analogous to – the
2739: present. Analogy underpins even the most mundane interpretation.
2740: Consider, for example, the way archaeologists assign function to
2741: objects: ‘this was a storage vessel’. We argue that it was a storage
2742: vessel in the past because its form (large, sturdy, undecorated) would
2743: make it seem ‘natural’ to us to use it in this way in the present.
2744: Further, we strengthen our argument through other analogies. The object
2745: was found with others of the same type in the room of an ancient palace
2746: adjacent to a food preparation area, which we label a ‘kitchen’ –
2747: another analogy with the present. Chemical analysis reveals the object
2748: contained food remains – again, by analogy with the present, we infer
2749: that the vessel was used to store food.
2750: 
2751: The more links we can make, the more we argue or assume that the two
2752: situations are analogous. This mode of analogical thinking is perhaps
2753: pretty obvious once it is spelt out in this manner, but nevertheless
2754: absolutely central to the way we write about the past. And like other
2755: such ‘obvious’ points, the issue of analogy needs further critical
2756: examination.
2757: 
2758: Binford and Middle-range Theory
2759: 
2760: Lewis Binford addressed this issue with great force in the 1970s. While
2761: the general arguments covered in the last chapter were raging over the
2762: grand theoretical issues of epistemology and positivism, Binford
2763: suggested that such debates were in many ways secondary to a more
2764: central issue. Binford insisted that archaeology’s claim to be a science
2765: rested or fell on this issue of analogy. His argument, in brief, ran
2766: like this:
2767: 
2768: Archaeological data – stones, bones, potsherds – form a static record in
2769: the present. We carefully record stones, bones, potsherds and their
2770: position and arrangement in the ground in the here and now. But we are
2771: not interested in the here and now, we are interested in the past: our
2772: task is to ask questions of this material in the present, questions
2773: about the past. Specifically we are interested in the dynamics of past
2774: societies, that is the way past cultural systems functioned, developed,
2775: were transformed.
2776: 
2777: Further, since science is a discipline which seeks to generalize for the
2778: reasons discussed in the last chapter, we want to develop generalizing
2779: theories about past dynamics (figure 4.1, which is a different version
2780: of figure 2.1).
2781: 
2782: All archaeologists offer possible links between statics and dynamics,
2783: every time they put forward an interpretation of archaeological
2784: evidence. In practice, Binford argued, archaeologists do this by making
2785: assumptions about the middle range, that is, the ‘space’ between statics
2786: and dynamics. For example, we excavate a cemetery consisting of a few
2787: graves with lots of grave-goods and many graves with very little (static
2788: data); from this we infer a society characterized by wealth or social
2789: inequality (past dynamics). We do so by assuming a middle-range link
2790: between the number and/or value of grave goods and the social/economic
2791: status of the person buried. A second example: we excavate a pueblo in
2792: the American Southwest and discover that it increases in size and number
2793: of rooms through time. We infer population increase from this. Again, we
2794: make a middle-range assumption, namely that settlement size, measured in
2795: terms of the number of rooms in a settlement, correlates positively with
2796: population size.
2797: 
2798: Right or wrong (and however obvious they may look at first sight, the
2799: assumptions in both of the examples above can be debated), these
2800: statements can be called middle-range assumptions. Such middle-range
2801: assumptions guide archaeologists from observation of the static
2802: archaeological record (burials in excavated cemeteries, data on
2803: settlements derived from surveys) to general statements and theories
2804: about the past (social structure and ranking, population estimates,
2805: possible links between these variables, generalizing propositions about
2806: links between rank, population size, and social complexity: figure 4.1).
2807: 
2808: Figure 4.1 Present statics, past dynamics and middle-range theory
2809: 
2810: [c04_image001.jpg]
2811: 
2812: Such propositions are called middle-range by Binford because they link,
2813: in his words, statics to dynamics, and particular observations of the
2814: archaeological record to general theories about the past. In much of
2815: ‘practical’ archaeology, Binford argues, many propositions are often not
2816: theorized and remain implicit, as they have an apparently
2817: common-sensical, banal or trivial nature: ‘the larger the settlement,
2818: the more people are likely to have lived there’.
2819: 
2820: Now many middle-range propositions may be banal or trivial, but any good
2821: scientist should make his or her disciplinary assumptions formal and
2822: explicit however obvious or common-sensical they might be. Many of the
2823: interpretive problems that arise within archaeological traditions that
2824: do not consider themselves to be ‘theoretical’ can be traced to a lack
2825: of clarity about middle-range assumptions, which are treated as ‘common
2826: sense’. For example, villages in medieval Europe may be referred to as
2827: ‘planned’ because they are identified as of regular design and are
2828: further seen as evidence of ‘lordship’. The implicit assumption that
2829: regularity (a static pattern, observable in the present) implies
2830: planning (a dynamic process in the past) and further implies ‘lordship’
2831: (a social institution) needs to be critically examined. Similar comments
2832: might be made about the assignation of high or low status to buildings
2833: on the basis of their size and decoration, or something so mundane as
2834: the designation of features as ‘roads’ or ‘trackways’ on the basis of
2835: their size and appearance.
2836: 
2837: If such assumptions remain implicit, untested, taken-for-granted, then
2838: we can never do more than tell untestable stories about the past,
2839: because the criteria for deciding that this argument is good and that
2840: argument is bad are never made clear, let alone objective and rigorous.
2841: One scholar can assert (as ‘atheoretical’ archaeologists frequently do)
2842: that this argument is ‘plausible’ and that other argument ‘wild’,
2843: ‘improbable’ or a ‘flight of fancy’ by reference to an implicit common
2844: sense which is never evaluated. Binford therefore suggested that we
2845: should consciously develop an explicit ‘middle-range theory’ (MRT) to
2846: link present statics with past dynamics. (It is important to note in
2847: passing that this archaeological definition of the middle-range is
2848: subtly different from that used by sociologists such as Robert Merton,
2849: who developed the term ‘theories of the middle range’ to delineate
2850: theories that fell in the range between empirical generalizations and
2851: ‘grand theory’.)
2852: 
2853: Now the only place that we can see a definite, certain, measurable link
2854: between a set of activity patterns or dynamics and what they look like
2855: archaeologically is in the present. We can never see a Neolithic farmer
2856: making a flint arrowhead with the debris flying in all directions, or a
2857: Palaeolithic hunter skinning and butchering an animal, leaving bones to
2858: kick around in the dust, be gnawed by dogs and finally be buried and
2859: enter the archaeological record. But what we can do is look at
2860: comparable activities – manufacturing of stone tools, hunting and
2861: butchering activities, the gathering, winnowing and sowing of seeds – in
2862: the present.
2863: 
2864: In the present, archaeologists can make a detailed and accurate record
2865: of how particular activities or systems of activities give rise to
2866: particular patterns of archaeological debris. We can look at how modern
2867: peasant societies prepare and process their grain and other foodstuffs,
2868: or how modern huntergatherers butcher their kills. We can observe and
2869: measure precisely what sort and how much debris then enters the
2870: archaeological record as a result of these activities, and what then
2871: happens to that debris as a result of attrition and decay.
2872: Archaeologists must therefore look at the ethnographic present for the
2873: source of an explicit middle-range theory. Binford wrote: ‘My aim was to
2874: study the relation between statics and dynamics in a modern setting. If
2875: understood in great detail, it would give us a kind of Rosetta Stone: a
2876: way of “translating” the static, material stone tools found on an
2877: archaeological site into the vibrant life of a group of people who in
2878: fact left them there’ (Binford 1983a: 24). In other words, just as the
2879: Rosetta Stone had the same inscription in three different ancient
2880: languages enabling the modern linguist to use the stone to translate
2881: from one language to another, so we should be trying to find things that
2882: enable us to translate from the observed archaeological record to the
2883: past.
2884: 
2885: Binford called such ethnographic studies actualistic; that is,
2886: observation and recording by archaeologists of ethnographic situations
2887: taking place in the actual, the here and now. He compared the
2888: archaeological record to that of a footprint in the forest. One might,
2889: seeing a particular size and shape of footprint, conjecture that it
2890: belonged to a large, heavy animal without having any clear idea of the
2891: animal concerned. Later, one observes a bear making that precise shape
2892: and form of a footprint; from that observation in the present, one can
2893: say with greater certainty that the previous footprint had been made by
2894: such an animal in the past.
2895: 
2896: One form of actualistic study that remains an enduring element of
2897: archaeological practice is experimental archaeology. For example,
2898: experimental studies have eludicated the manufacture and use of flaked
2899: and polished stone tools. The debris produced at each stage of
2900: experimental manufacture could be compared with the debris in the
2901: archaeological record; microscopic traces of wear on prehistoric tools
2902: could be compared with those on modern tools that had been used
2903: experimentally for butchering, scraping hides and so on. Actualistic
2904: study can also take the form of ethnoarchaeology, or the study of
2905: material culture in the present by archaeologists.
2906: 
2907: Archaeologists have always been interested in the study of modern
2908: peoples and their material culture, and have used ethnographic material
2909: as analogies for things they saw in the archaeological record. Long
2910: before David Clarke wrote about the Iron Age lake village of
2911: Glastonbury, the huts there had been compared to huts of ‘natives’ in
2912: East Africa. Indeed, such parallels were at the heart of the emergence
2913: of archaeology as a discipline. Nineteenth-century ideas of social
2914: evolution, in which all societies went through the same fundamental
2915: stages, carried the implication that we could use present day
2916: ‘primitive’ societies as analogies for the prehistoric past. Thus, for
2917: example, Lubbock’s famous 1865 book Prehistoric Times was subtitled As
2918: illustrated by ancient remains, and the manners and customs of modern
2919: savages. It contained illustrations of artefacts from both prehistory
2920: and from contemporary groups such as the Inuit.
2921: 
2922: Binford and others, however, suggested that such ‘ethnoarchaeological’
2923: work had to be done primarily not by ethnographers, but by
2924: archaeologists with a specific eye to the analogies between present and
2925: past. Archaeologists could not rely on ethnographers collecting relevant
2926: material, since their aims and purposes were different – ethnographers
2927: were concerned with a range of issues, and were not in Binford’s view
2928: specifically interested in the link between human activities and
2929: archaeological material.
2930: 
2931: For Binford, the development of ‘robust’ middle-range theory was far
2932: more important than metaphysical debates about the status of archaeology
2933: as a science. While colleagues were arguing about the finer points of
2934: philosophy and epistemology, then, Binford set off for Alaska to study
2935: the Nunamiut ‘Eskimo’ hunters.
2936: 
2937: Interpreting the Mousterian
2938: 
2939: Binford’s account of why he did so (related most accessibly in In
2940: Pursuit of the Past) is instructive. Binford had been interested in the
2941: ‘Mousterian Question’ for some time. The Mousterian period at the
2942: junction of the Middle and Upper Palaeolithic could be found in
2943: different contexts in the Old World, but had been most closely analysed
2944: in southern France; it was named after the French cave site of Le
2945: Moustier.
2946: 
2947: Figure 4.2 Bordes’ Mousterian assemblage types, as redrawn by Binford
2948: (1983a)
2949: 
2950: [c04_image002.jpg]
2951: 
2952: The Mousterian was defined and characterized by certain distinctive
2953: types of stone tools, which had been classified by the French
2954: archaeologist François Bordes. Bordes had then looked at the different
2955: proportions of different stone tool types in different layers, and shown
2956: that these could be classified into different patterns or assemblages:
2957: ‘typical Mousterian’, ‘Charentian’, ‘Mousterian of Acheulean tradition’,
2958: ‘denticulate Mousterian’, and so on (figure 4.2).
2959: 
2960: How might we interpret this pattern found in the present in terms of
2961: past processes? Bordes had assumed that different stone tool assemblages
2962: reflected the presence of different cultural groups. That is, he
2963: followed the assumptions of other traditional or culture-historical
2964: archaeologists in assuming that certain artefact types and assemblages
2965: represented distinctive ‘cultures’. Different stone tool types reflected
2966: different cultural norms, and assemblage variability therefore reflected
2967: cultural variability. Others disagreed in how this ‘interassemblage
2968: variability’ might be interpreted. Binford, for example, suggested that
2969: different assemblages were not the detritus of different cultures, but
2970: should be seen within an adaptive framework – as different ‘tool-kits’.
2971: 
2972: Binford wanted to test his hypothesis. He collected huge numbers of
2973: statistics from Mousterian assemblages in France and put them on to a
2974: computer. (We see here the stress on grasping variability and on
2975: quantitative methods characteristic of New Archaeology.) He expended a
2976: huge amount of labour feeding the information in, and found that there
2977: were some interesting patterns in the material he had collected. But
2978: herein lay the central problem. Binford found that there was nothing he
2979: could do with them.
2980: 
2981: Binford had no secure idea what activities or processes might have
2982: created those patterns. Bordes’s suggestion that different proportions
2983: of tool types were reflective of different cultural groups, Binford’s
2984: argument that different groups of tools were different ‘tool-kits’, and
2985: reflected different kinds of cultural adaptation, and other Palaeolithic
2986: specialists’ interpretations might all be appropriate explanations, all
2987: of which appeared to be ‘satisfactory’ explanations of the data, no
2988: matter how many statistics the computer churned out.
2989: 
2990: Using Binford’s own terminology, he had lots of information about
2991: statics in the present, but lacked the means – the middle-range theory –
2992: to translate these into past dynamics. As a result his ideas of what his
2993: patterns meant could be contrasted with the ideas of others such as
2994: Bordes, without being able to test between the two. The arguments of
2995: Binford and Bordes remained just different stories, with no clear way to
2996: judge which was the more accurate.
2997: 
2998: Binford’s work with the Nunamiut, then, was animated by a desire to
2999: produce middle-range information that would give him a ‘handle’ on this
3000: question. The Nunamiut, as a group who practised hunting of deer in an
3001: environment similar to that which pertained in southern France during
3002: the Mousterian period, offered a chance to attack the question of what
3003: these patterns might mean. Observation of the Nunamiut could answer the
3004: question: ‘what kinds of hunter-gatherer activities give rise to what
3005: kinds of archaeological assemblages?’
3006: 
3007: Before we can consider whether Binford’s Nunamiut work gave him this
3008: secure handle, we must consider its underlying theory a little more.
3009: 
3010: Uniformitarian Assumptions
3011: 
3012: Binford lays down two conditions that middle-range theory must satisfy.
3013: Middle-range theory must be:
3014: 
3015: 1 Formally independent from our development of general theory. Remember
3016: that in the positivist view, science is about testing things, and the
3017: importance of keeping method separate from theory. How can
3018: archaeologists test between two general theories if our middle-range
3019: theory is based on one of them? This would give rise to the danger of a
3020: circular argument.
3021: 
3022: 2 Based on a uniformitarian assumption. In other words, archaeologists
3023: have to assume that conditions in the past were like those in the
3024: present. If conditions in the past varied, all bets are off; anything
3025: could have happened; actualistic studies in the present offer no secure
3026: guide to what might have happened in the past.
3027: 
3028: Consider this second assumption for a moment. Archaeologists can assume
3029: that physical properties and processes (gravity, the structure of
3030: molecules, geological processes such as soil formation and
3031: sedimentation) were the same in the present as in the past. Indeed, it
3032: was this assumption that was central to the development of modern
3033: geology, and hence stratigraphy in archaeology, in the nineteenth
3034: century. The nineteenth-century geologist Lyell had argued against
3035: ascribing geological features such as layers of sedimentary rock to
3036: catastrophes and cataclysmic events such as the biblical Flood, events
3037: that had no parallel in the present. Instead, we had to assume that
3038: features such as layers of rock were created by processes observable in
3039: the present (such as marine sedimentation). Of course, a few physical
3040: processes and conditions are demonstrably not uniform – the proportion
3041: of carbon-14 in the atmosphere has varied over the millennia, for
3042: example, which is why carbon-14 determinations have to be ‘calibrated’
3043: against dendrochnology in order to turn determinations into dates.
3044: 
3045: Physical processes may have largely been the same in the past, but human
3046: behaviour is much more diverse. Uniformitarian assumptions about human
3047: behaviour are much more difficult to make. The law of gravity applies
3048: regardless of time and place, but different human cultures do things in
3049: very different ways. This difficulty is particularly apparent for the
3050: Lower and Middle Palaeolithic periods where archaeologists are dealing
3051: with extinct hominid species that are not Homo sapiens sapiens. We can
3052: debate whether ‘human nature’ or ‘basic human needs’ are constant for
3053: other periods, but at these early times we are dealing with hominid
3054: species that are not our own. Archaeologists cannot therefore assume
3055: that practices we might suppose common to all human groups were even
3056: present at that time. Binford himself makes the point that practices
3057: such as ‘home-base behaviour’ (regular return to a ‘home’ such as a
3058: transitory camp) may be ‘natural’ or assumed for modern humans, but
3059: cannot be assumed for the hominids of the Lower Palaeolithic.
3060: 
3061: Somewhere in the middle, between the physical and human worlds, are
3062: assumptions about the behaviour of animals and plants, resting in the
3063: domain of ecology. Plant and animal ecology is not quite as ‘hard’ and
3064: invariate as the laws of physics and chemistry, but not as ‘soft’ and
3065: variable as cultural behaviour. For example, modern sheep will herd when
3066: threatened by a predator, whereas other species will scatter; in so far
3067: as this behaviour has a genetic basis, this has obvious implications for
3068: which species were easier to domesticate in the Neolithic (sheep being
3069: amenable to being herded by humans and dogs).
3070: 
3071: It follows that the development of middle-range theory may be relatively
3072: plausible for areas of the archaeological record that are governed by or
3073: dependent on physical or biological processes, but less so in other
3074: areas. Let’s consider some examples:
3075: 
3076: 1 The preparation of grain for human consumption can only be performed
3077: in certain ways: harvesting can only take place during certain times of
3078: the year, the wheat must be separated from the chaff, the grain must be
3079: dried, and so on. The different processes involved in the harvesting and
3080: processing of grain give rise to certain recognizable by-products like
3081: chaff or charred remains. The processing of crops can readily be
3082: explored through ethnoarchaeology and experimental archaeology (figure
3083: 4.3).
3084: 
3085: 2 Ageing of animals. Archaeologists can assume that measures of animal
3086: age such as tooth eruption sequences did not vary in the past, and
3087: further assume that mating and breeding patterns took place in the same
3088: seasonal rhythm. It is therefore possible to take a collection of bones
3089: and determine not only age at death, but at what time in the year
3090: animals were being killed. This in turn might lead to secure inferences
3091: about whether animals were being bred for meat or dairy products, and so
3092: on.
3093: 
3094: 3 As a more marginal example to explore the limits of the uniformitarian
3095: assumption, we can differentiate between ‘kill sites’ and ‘residential
3096: sites’ on the basis of the bones found at each site. Archaeologists can
3097: safely make the uniformitarian assumption that the ratio of meat to bone
3098: remains fairly constant for an animal’s body part, and therefore reason
3099: that hunters will carry back the meat-bearing parts of the animal while
3100: leaving behind less ‘meaty’ parts of the animal (something often
3101: referred to as the ‘Schlepp effect’). Whether a site is a kill site or a
3102: home base will therefore be reflected in the different kinds of bone
3103: left on site, and much ethnoarchaeological research has gone into
3104: establishing just how this might work. (But this might not work because
3105: of ‘cultural’ factors. A group might have cultural taboos against
3106: consuming a particular part of an animal, or conversely a part might be
3107: prized for its ‘ritual’ properties. A high cultural value placed on part
3108: of an animal that did not correspond to its calorific or protein value
3109: might confuse these signatures. I shall explore this problem further
3110: below.)
3111: 
3112: Figure 4.3 Part of Hillman’s (1984) ethnoarchaeological model of grain
3113: processing, derived from ethnographic research in Turkey, with F
3114: indicating those points at which products are exposed to fire and
3115: therefore preservation by charring. After Hillman (1984: figure 2)
3116: 
3117: [c04_image003.jpg]
3118: 
3119: Many of these studies can be grouped under the heading of taphonomy.
3120: Taphonomy is the study of how the archaeological record is created from
3121: ‘cultural’ and ‘natural’ behaviour. It stresses how we cannot make easy
3122: connections between what we find in the archaeological record and past
3123: activities, and is most strongly developed in areas like botanical and
3124: faunal remains and animal behaviour.
3125: 
3126: To take one small example: the gnawing of bones by dogs. Sebastian Payne
3127: has shown how gnawed bones will more easily decay than complete bones.
3128: Sites, therefore, on which dogs are present may have many fewer bones,
3129: and perhaps different sorts of bones, than sites where no dogs are
3130: present. The negative conclusion of this is that we may misinterpret the
3131: very similar economy of sites whose only real difference is that dogs
3132: are present at one of them.
3133: 
3134: Case Study: Bones at Olduvai
3135: 
3136: To be successful, then, middle-range theory must not only develop
3137: propositions linking present statics and past dynamics. It must then
3138: take those propositions and use them in a formal way to judge between
3139: different interpretations of the same archaeological assemblage. Let us
3140: look at a case study to see whether such a judgement actually happens.
3141: 
3142: In the last chapter of his book Bones: Ancient Men and Modern Myths,
3143: Binford claims that ‘my analysis will represent the application of a
3144: methodology developed from control information obtained in actualistic
3145: studies’ (1981b: 253). In other words, he will use his
3146: ethnoarchaeological work to help determine which process gave rise to
3147: which pattern in the archaeological record. Does it?
3148: 
3149: Binford develops a series of propositions from his Nunamiut material,
3150: thereby predicting the static results of certain dynamics. For example,
3151: he proposes that if tool-using hominids were living in base camps,
3152: hunting and killing animals and transporting some parts of the kill,
3153: then one would expect a home-base assemblage similar to that of modern
3154: hunter-gatherer residences. Among other things, one would expect the
3155: hominids to be bringing back parts of the animal with lots of meat, and
3156: leaving behind parts with little meat. If, on the other hand, the
3157: hominids are only scavenging, other hunting animals will have taken off
3158: the high meat-bearing parts, leaving only low-utility parts for the
3159: hominids to pick over, resulting in an inverse situation.
3160: 
3161: Binford then looks at different sites, dividing them into sites where
3162: the animal caracasses have also been scavenged by other animals and
3163: sites where this has not happened (‘ravaged’ and ‘non-ravaged’). He
3164: treats unravaged sites in detail, arguing against the association of
3165: hominids with the animal remains. He concludes that there are some less
3166: noticed assemblages ‘consistent with the removal of essentially
3167: marrow-yielding bones from the already ravaged kills of other
3168: predator-scavengers’, an interpretation reinforced by stone tool
3169: evidence.
3170: 
3171: In my view, this is an interesting and logical argument, but not a
3172: formal example of the application of middle-range theory. One would
3173: expect a series of formal propositions correlating statics with
3174: dynamics, then an observation of the statics, and finally a formal
3175: analogy with the dynamics. (To pursue Binford’s own analogy, first we
3176: propose that bears leave certain prints, then see a print, and conclude
3177: that it was made by a bear.) But Binford does not do this. Instead, the
3178: evidence is explored contextually, with the Nunamiut work serving in a
3179: looser role than that of formal analogy. For example, the wolf-kill
3180: parallel is not even mentioned in the initial set of formal
3181: propositions. So Binford’s use of ethnoarchaeological data is
3182: interesting and even rigorous, but not a formal use of middle-range
3183: theory if we adopt the strict criteria that Binford himself laid down.
3184: 
3185: We might extend these observations to the Mousterian case study outlined
3186: above. It might be expected after Binford’s work that secure inferences
3187: could be drawn about how the Mousterian might be interpreted. But in
3188: fact the debate has simply gone on, or one might argue in more positive
3189: terms that it has progressed and expanded its terms of reference. The
3190: conclusion we can draw from this is that while middle-range research can
3191: help us think about the archaeological record in new and interesting
3192: ways, it is difficult to see it working in practice to formally judge
3193: between alternative hypotheses in a way that will ‘prove’ one theory and
3194: ‘disprove’ another. Rather, as Binford himself came to insist in his
3195: later work, middle-range research can act to expand and deepen our
3196: explanations by identifying ambiguity in the archaeological record, and
3197: by pointing towards avenues of further research to define and explore
3198: that ambiguity. What other reasons are there to be sceptical about the
3199: value of middle-range theory?
3200: 
3201: Middle-range Theory: Problems
3202: 
3203: In my view, Binford is quite accurate in suggesting that archaeology
3204: stands or falls as a positivist ‘science’ as conceived in the last
3205: chapter on the successful development of middle-range theory. If we can
3206: reliably relate present statics to past dynamics through the use of
3207: middle-range propositions, then we really can isolate archaeological
3208: reasoning from its social and political context and stop just telling
3209: stories about the past. If we cannot do so, then the aim of developing
3210: archaeology as a neutral science in the narrowly positivist sense might
3211: appear doomed.
3212: 
3213: Two issues can be raised with respect to the debate over analogy and
3214: middle-range theory:
3215: 
3216: (1) An analogy, whether based on the rubric of middle-range theory or
3217: not, doesn’t prove or test anything. We can never know whether the
3218: uniformitarian asumption discussed above is in fact correct. If I
3219: interpret an excavated feature as a storage pit, and show that the
3220: feature shares 5, 10, 15 characteristics in common with ethnographically
3221: ‘known’ storage pits, there is still a possibility that the feature
3222: could be interpreted in some other way.
3223: 
3224: This criticism gets stronger the more sceptical one is about general
3225: theories of cultural evolution. If human cultures really do all go
3226: through the same stages, and cultures at each stage of cultural
3227: evolution are basically similar to one another, it follows that features
3228: from excavated, prehistoric contexts will probably be very like those
3229: known from ethnography. A uniformitarian assumption based on cultural
3230: similarity will therefore be very strong. To put it another way, if the
3231: Inuit really are just like all hunter-gatherer groups, then the use of
3232: their material culture and patterns of activity as an analogy for past
3233: hunter-gatherer groups is very plausible.
3234: 
3235: If on the other hand one believes that all cultures are historically
3236: unique, and cannot be compared one with another or lumped into
3237: evolutionary stages, then there is no reason to accept that this feature
3238: was a storage pit merely because it looks like one from another culture
3239: 2000 years later, 3000 miles away and from a completely different
3240: cultural context. Perhaps we should treat the Inuit as unique or at
3241: least distinctive, and if so, be very cautious about arguing that any
3242: patterns derived from their means of living can be used in any way as a
3243: template for any other hunter-gatherer group past or present. We might
3244: certainly point to the fact that groups such as the !Kung San are not
3245: ‘pristine’ hunting and gathering groups, but have complex histories of
3246: their own. Here is a case where it can be argued that middle-range
3247: assumptions are not in fact independent of general theory; on this
3248: argument, they are inextricably linked to general theories, in this case
3249: views of cultural evolution.
3250: 
3251: Advocates of middle-range theory would reply, following from the last
3252: chapter, that it does not matter where ideas come from – what matters is
3253: how they are tested. Of course we shall never be absolutely sure that
3254: this feature was a storage pit, but we can evaluate its plausibility
3255: against alternative hypotheses.
3256: 
3257: In his later work, Binford suggests that we don’t absolutely test a
3258: proposition one way or the other, but we can focus on where the
3259: archaeological record is ambiguous. In other words, archaeologists
3260: should focus on the lack of fit between the data and what they expected
3261: to find, and the things in the archaeological record that we cannot
3262: easily explain. These elements of ambiguity and lack of fit, he argues,
3263: generate fresh research questions and prompt further research. Over
3264: time, then, a scientific archaeology will never provide a full, final
3265: explanation, but it can and does demonstrably move forward in its
3266: understanding of past processes, as in the wolf-kill example discussed
3267: above.
3268: 
3269: An analogy can be strengthened if some form of cultural continuity can
3270: be demonstrated between two groups. It has been argued for example that
3271: the modern Hopi tribe of the southwestern United States are the cultural
3272: descendants of what archaeologists have called ‘Anasazi’ populations.
3273: Now on ‘Anasazi’ sites archaeologists find semi-subterranean circular
3274: chambers, very similar to the ones found on modern Hopi pueblos. It is,
3275: therefore, plausible to refer to modern Hopi rituals in their own kivas.
3276: Again, such a link does not conclusively ‘prove’ anything, but it does
3277: derive interpretative strength from such a link because continuity in
3278: cultural ideas and practices is probable.
3279: 
3280: An important element of traditional archaeology in North America was the
3281: direct historical method, where one started by delineating groups of
3282: Native Americans in the present and then tried to trace their cultural
3283: antecedents to, effectively, prehistoric groups. Such a method has no
3284: direct parallel in European archaeology, though Christopher Hawkes once
3285: suggested we should write prehistory backwards, working from ‘known’
3286: historical groups back to the Iron and Bronze Ages. Such an approach
3287: also underlies recent European interest in ‘ethnogenesis’, or the study
3288: of the creation of ethnic identities. Scholars work backwards from
3289: ‘known’ cultural or ethnic groups in the historical or folklife records,
3290: combining the evidence of linguistics, archaeology, and ethnography, to
3291: examine the ‘genesis’ of these groups in prehistory.
3292: 
3293: Both Alison Wylie and Ian Hodder have stressed a distinction between
3294: formal and relational analogies, suggesting that the latter are
3295: stronger. Formal analogies rest simply on the notion that if some
3296: elements of the two situations are similar, others must be also.
3297: Obviously such analogies are weak, though they tend to be stronger the
3298: more points of similarity that can be demonstrated between the two
3299: contexts. Relational analogies rest on a cultural or natural connection
3300: between the two contexts, as with the direct historical method, where
3301: connections based on cultural continuity can be suggested.
3302: 
3303: For example, we might interpret pits on African prehistoric sites as
3304: grain storage pits, citing a range of ethnographic examples. As it
3305: stands, such an analogy is pretty weak, though it might be strengthened
3306: with the number and range of parallels we might draw (are the pits of
3307: the same size and shape? Do we consider past and present societies to be
3308: at the ‘same level of social development’? Do the ethnographic examples
3309: come from the same kind of environment, settlement type, economy?). An
3310: understanding of the relations between different variables would make
3311: the analogies still stronger. Do the ethnographic examples come from
3312: African societies with direct historical links to the prehistoric
3313: culture we are studying? Can we ask why grain is stored in this way,
3314: citing ‘natural’ reasons (the most efficient way to keep grain dry in
3315: this type of climate) or ‘cultural’ factors?
3316: 
3317: In practice, of course, formal and relational analogies are two ends of
3318: a range of analogical arguments of lesser or greater strength.
3319: 
3320: (2) The issue of cultural continuity leads on to the second issue. It
3321: can be argued that people are influenced by cultural ideas in their
3322: behaviour. These ideas don’t just affect obviously ‘cultural’ things
3323: like religious beliefs manifested in the manner of burial or other
3324: ‘ritual’ practices. They also affect apparently mundane activities such
3325: as the organization of household space and attitudes towards rubbish.
3326: They therefore affect archaeological deposition, and must be taken into
3327: account when considering the formation of the archaeological record.
3328: 
3329: Take for example Henrietta Moore’s ethnoarchaeological study of the
3330: Marakwet in East Africa. Moore found that the way Marakwet houses and
3331: compounds were laid out related to their ideas of gender – what it
3332: meant, culturally, to be a man or to be a woman in Marakwet society.
3333: Different areas of domestic compounds were thought of as ‘male’ and
3334: ‘female’. This set of cultural ideas affected where individual
3335: residences lay and how the compound was laid out. Again, Ian Hodder
3336: looked at the distribution of pig and cattle bones in different
3337: settlements of the Nuba. He found lots of pig bones in the compounds of
3338: one tribe, but very few in another. Hodder suggested that this was due
3339: to different cultural attitudes. In this culture, women were associated
3340: with pigs – women were responsible for the pigs’ feeding and
3341: maintenance. In one area, there was a strong (male) belief that women
3342: were polluting: hence, by association, compounds were kept clean of pig
3343: bones and other debris. In another area, such pollution beliefs were
3344: weak, the people of that area seemed not to care that their compounds
3345: were left covered in refuse. Hodder argued that an archaeologist
3346: excavating different compounds would need to understand something of
3347: Nuba beliefs in order to interpret the faunal remains ‘correctly’.
3348: 
3349: Much ethnoarchaeological and experimental work continues on the
