3350: relationship between statics and dynamics. A particularly strong area is
3351: the taphonomy of bones, but other areas include the production of stone
3352: tools, plant remains and site economy in general. Such work tends to
3353: concentrate on problems raised by the archaeological record of the
3354: Palaeolithic and early farming communities – in other words, relatively
3355: early elements of the archaeological record. It tries to focus on quite
3356: specific archaeological questions, often relating to subsistence
3357: economy.
3358: 
3359: Behavioural Archaeology
3360: 
3361: Let’s return to figure 2.1, the gap between present and past, and the
3362: issue of how to infer past processes from archaeological data in the
3363: present.
3364: 
3365: A second method to bridge this gap was outlined by Michael Schiffer, who
3366: coined the term behavioural archaeology. Behavioural archaeology began
3367: in the 1970s as the study of site formation processes – how artefacts
3368: move from their ‘systemic context’, that is, their use in actual human
3369: behaviour, to their ‘archaeological context’ in which they are
3370: excavated. Schiffer drew attention to the intervening factors between
3371: these two contexts: for example, ‘depositional processes’, ‘reclamation
3372: processes’, ‘disturbance processes’ and ‘reuse processes’. In seeking to
3373: link up behaviour with patterning in the archaeological record, the
3374: project of behavioural archaeology obviously shares common ground with
3375: much of middle-range theory and taphonomy. Binford himself rejected this
3376: term and chose to attack much of Schiffer’s philosophy in quite
3377: outspoken terms; but behavioural archaeology, taphonomy and middle-range
3378: theory can be considered as closely related bodies of thinking.
3379: 
3380: Behavioural archaeology, however, goes on from this point to encompass a
3381: much broader set of interests, and to define itself as a distinctive
3382: approach to archaeology as a whole. Skibo and Schiffer (2008, 6) have
3383: stated:
3384: 
3385: the core component of this approach is the redefinition of archaeology
3386: as a discipline that studies relationships between people and things in
3387: all times and all places … The relationships between people and
3388: artifacts are discussed in terms of regularities discerned in processes
3389: of manufacture, use, and disposal that make up the life histories of
3390: material things, as in flow models and behavioural chains.
3391: 
3392: The most famous example of behavioural archaeology is the Tucson Garbage
3393: Project initiated by Bill Rathje, where the link between behaviour and
3394: material debris is examined in the present through study of the garbage
3395: the modern people of Tucson throw away.
3396: 
3397: In other words, behavioural archaeology looks for regularities in the
3398: relations between people and objects in all times and places. In this
3399: way, it brings together study of the prehistoric past with studies of
3400: much more recent artefacts, including objects in the present. Some of
3401: Schiffer’s most fascinating work has been on objects such as the early
3402: electric car, the lighthouse, and the portable radio, which sits
3403: alongside that of his and colleagues’ studies of more ‘traditional’
3404: topics such as pottery manufacture and the discard and reuse of objects.
3405: 
3406: Material Culture Studies
3407: 
3408: Behavioural archaeology has also come to share a set of interests with
3409: ‘material culture studies’ in general. Ethnoarchaeological work such as
3410: Henrietta Moore’s and Ian Hodder’s, mentioned above, led to the
3411: proposition that to understand material culture, one had to look at
3412: cultural meanings. The way was thus laid open to a range of studies
3413: which looked at material objects, buildings and landscapes in both the
3414: prehistoric and recent past and present, in which the methods of
3415: archaeology, ethnography and related areas were combined.
3416: 
3417: It is often difficult to see any distinction in this work between
3418: archaeology and anthropology – whether, that is, scholars are looking
3419: archaeologically at material culture in its social context, or
3420: anthropologically at society with a theoretical emphasis on the
3421: importance of materiality and material things. Indeed, an
3422: interdisciplinary journal edited by this ‘school’ (the Journal of
3423: Material Culture) brings together different disciplines in this very
3424: way, and includes work by art and design historians, scholars from
3425: cultural studies, archaeologists, cultural anthropologists, and many
3426: other areas.
3427: 
3428: It can also be argued that these interests have very deep historical
3429: roots they go back to the importance attached to the collection of
3430: material culture by early ethnographers, giving rise to many of the
3431: great collections of artefacts found in museums like the Pitt Rivers or
3432: the great North American collections such as the Smithsonian. They also
3433: go back to the origins of archaeology in early modern antiquarianism and
3434: its emphasis on the close study and discussion of monuments and
3435: artefacts from a wide range of areas and periods.
3436: 
3437: A particularly important element of recent work in material culture
3438: studies has been the archaeology of the recent past. Scholars have
3439: looked, for example, at the material traces of the Second World War and
3440: of the Cold War, combining written history, the testimony of
3441: participants, and archaeological study of surviving structures and
3442: artefacts. Much of this work brings together interdisciplinary interests
3443: such as a concern with how people’s memories and accounts of the past
3444: are embedded in material objects and landscapes.
3445: 
3446: As a result of this convergence of approaches and interests, the study
3447: of material culture in the present, allied to a recasting of archaeology
3448: as not simply about the past but about the relationship of people and
3449: things, is one of the most important and vibrant areas of archaeological
3450: theory today. There are a diversity of approaches drawing theoretical
3451: inspiration from a diversity of sources, from a continuing tradition of
3452: middle-range research through to studies of modern material culture
3453: informed by postmodern and interpretive theories. I will come back to
3454: these approaches again and again in future chapters, and try to pull
3455: them together at the end of the book under the general banner of
3456: ‘materiality’.
3457: 
3458: Which approach to material objects offers the most convincing way
3459: forward depends, in part, on the general theory of society that one
3460: finds convincing. Can one generalize between societies? Are societies to
3461: be seen as systems fundamentally adapted to their environment, or is
3462: symbolic meaning important? These are questions we will examine in the
3463: next chapter.
3464: 
3465: 5
3466:
